coredata.dc:title: A simple turn on fluorescent sensor for the selective detection of thiamine using coconut water derived luminescent carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Purbia, Rahul
Paria, Santanu
coredata.dc:description:
In this study microwave-assisted hydrothermal method was used to prepare highly luminescent carbon dots (1–6nm size) within a minute from tender coconut (Cocos nucifera) water. The synthesized carbon dots (C-dots) exhibit emission of blue and green lights while excited at 390 and 450nm wavelengths, respectively. As an application, these C-dots were tested for a simple “turn on” fluorescent sensor for rapid detection of thiamine (vitamin B1). The detection of thiamine in human body is very important to prevent various diseases such as beriberi, neurological disorders, optic neuropathy, etc. The fluorescence emission intensity of C-dots quenches after addition of Cu2+ ion and then again increases selectively (turn on) after the addition of thiamine. The fluorescence emission intensity enhancement of Cu2+ ion modified C-dots in the presence of thiamine exhibits a linear relationship within the thiamine concentration range of 10–50μM. The limit of detection was found to be 280nM from this study. The selectivity of the detection was also tested in the presence of different organic molecules and inorganic ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Cl−,
SO
4
2 -
, and
NO
3
-
) which are present in blood serum and urine and found to be almost no interference in the detection. Finally, to see the applicability in real samples a commercial vitamin capsule was tested and found less than 3% error in the detected concentration. The C-dots were also used for bioimaging of fungus and the results show they are also suitable for this application too.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Tender coconut water
Microwave-hydrothermal
Photoluminescence
Thiamine sensor
Bioimaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst
xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and optical characterization of C-dots
C-dots based fluorometric detection of thiamine and selectivity test

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of as-prepared C-dots at different temperatures (a) 140°C (b) 160°C (c) 180°C (d) closer look of C-dots synthesized at 180°C.

XRD pattern of C-dots synthesized at 180°C for 1min (inset 20min) (a), FT-IR spectra of C-dots and coconut water (b), Raman spectra of as synthesized C-dots at different temperatures: 140°C for 1min (c), 160°C for 1min (d), 180°C for 1min (e), 180°C for 20min (f).
UV–vis absorption spectra of as-prepared C-dots at different temperatures.

Excitation dependent emission spectra of C-dots prepared at different temperatures: (a) 130°C, (b) 140°C, (c) 150°C, (d) 160°C, (e) 170°C, (f). 180°C. Photograph of the C-dots suspension shows blue (λ ex 390nm) and green (λ ex 450nm) color emission (right side).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots under different conditions, (b) the fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots-Cu2+ in the presence of thiamine (10–50μM). (c) The standard calibration curve for detection of thiamine was obtained between ΔF and concentration of thiamine. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for three replicate measurements at 33.27, 67.45, 101.18, 134.90, and 168.63µg/L (10–50μM) concentrations of thiamine were ±1.41, ±1.46, ±1.33, ±1.06, and ±0.85% respectively. (d) Zeta potentials of the C-dots, C-dots+Cu2+, C-dots+Cu2++thiamine with RSD values±1.41%, ±2.11%, and 1.86% respectively.

(a) Schematic illustration of the fluorescence response of C-dots in the presence of Cu2+ and thiamine. The fluorescence imaging of (b) C-dots, (c) C-dots+Cu2+ and (d) C-dots+Cu2++thiamine treated fungus cells excited at 490 nm wavelength. (All images contains 20μm scale bar).

head.ce:title: A simple turn on fluorescent sensor for the selective detection of thiamine using coconut water derived luminescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Interfaces and Nanomaterials Laboratory, Department of Chemical Engineering, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela 769008, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Interfaces and Nanomaterials Laboratory, Department of Chemical Engineering, National Institute of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this study microwave-assisted hydrothermal method was used to prepare highly luminescent carbon dots (1–6nm size) within a minute from tender coconut (Cocos nucifera) water. The synthesized carbon dots (C-dots) exhibit emission of blue and green lights while excited at 390 and 450nm wavelengths, respectively. As an application, these C-dots were tested for a simple “turn on” fluorescent sensor for rapid detection of thiamine (vitamin B1). The detection of thiamine in human body is very important to prevent various diseases such as beriberi, neurological disorders, optic neuropathy, etc. The fluorescence emission intensity of C-dots quenches after addition of Cu2+ ion and then again increases selectively (turn on) after the addition of thiamine. The fluorescence emission intensity enhancement of Cu2+ ion modified C-dots in the presence of thiamine exhibits a linear relationship within the thiamine concentration range of 10–50μM. The limit of detection was found to be 280nM from this study. The selectivity of the detection was also tested in the presence of different organic molecules and inorganic ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Cl−,
SO
4
2 -
, and
NO
3
-
) which are present in blood serum and urine and found to be almost no interference in the detection. Finally, to see the applicability in real samples a commercial vitamin capsule was tested and found less than 3% error in the detected concentration. The C-dots were also used for bioimaging of fungus and the results show they are also suitable for this application too.

ce:section.ce:para: Different nanostructures of carbon such as carbon nanotubes (De Volder et al., 2013), graphene (Zhu et al., 2010), nanofibers (De Jong and Geus, 2000), fullerene (Diederich and Gómez-López, 1999), nano-diamonds (Mochalin et al., 2012), graphene quantum dots (Bacon et al., 2013) are attracting increasing attention of nanotechnologists during the past few decades, because of their excellent physicochemical properties. Owing to this fact, carbon-based nanostructured materials are still at the center of nanoscale research and expanding into multifarious directions. Recently, C-dots have attracted tremendous attention because of their enormous promising applications in the fields of sensing, fluorescent ink, catalysis, bio-imaging, drug delivery, solar, and optoelectronic devices owing to their unique optical properties (Li et al., 2012; Luo et al., 2013). Fluorescent C-dots with tunable and strong luminescence properties are considered to be the next generation's green nanomaterials to replace conventionally used fluoroprobes, such as organic dyes, conjugated polymer, quantum dots, up-converting nanoparticles. The excellent properties of C-dots in terms of photostability, chemically inert, cost effective, nontoxic, eco-friendly, biocompatible, colloidal stability, and the tunable photoluminescence makes them more suitable for many novel applications than that of conventional fluoroprobes (Demchenko and Dekaliuk, 2013).

Recently, several strategies have been developed for the synthesis and functionalization of C-dots, such as arc discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, thermal combustion, acidic oxidation, microwave pyrolysis, hydrothermal oxidation of different carbonaceous sources, and so on (Baker and Baker, 2010; Li et al., 2012). Most of these approaches often suffer from serious bottlenecking such as multistep fabrication, complicated setup, expensive starting material and process, post separation technique, required strong acidic environment, low yield, time consuming process, and surface passivation. On the other hand, green synthesis of fluorescent C-dots using natural precursors such as carbohydrates (sucrose, glucose) (Zhang et al., 2010b; Peng and Travas-Sejdic, 2009), glycerol (Lai et al., 2012), banana juice (De and Karak, 2013), food waste (Park et al., 2014), ascorbic acid (Zhang et al., 2010a), citric acid (Krysmann et al., 2012), candle soot (Liu et al., 2007), chitosan (Chowdhury et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2012), grass (Liu et al., 2012), coffee grounds (Hsu et al., 2012), soy milk (Zhu et al., 2012), egg (Wang et al., 2012) etc. are getting more attention, which may essentially replace the conventional chemical routes. However, most of these methods usually also involve a complex process, multi-step fabrication, pre and post separation, aggregation of the particle, and strong acidic conditions. Therefore, easy and simple green route synthesis of photoluminescent C-dots without surface passivation has lots of importance in various practical applications.

While summarizing the literature on applications of C-dots, it has been found that significant efforts have been given on bioimaging as well detection of different bio and inorganic molecules. Among several important bio-molecules, easy detection of thiamine (vitamin B1) is very important. Thiamine is a sulfur-containing water soluble vitamin which is naturally produced by bacteria, plants, and fungi. Thiamine deficiency causes various diseases in the human body such as beriberi, neurological disorders, and optic neuropathy, etc. The easy and low-level detection of thiamine is useful to prevent these diseases at the early stage as well as important in the pharmaceutical industries. To date, the fluorescent determination of thiamine with various material such as Au NPs (Rao and Paria, 2014), Eu-doped Y2O3 (Moghaddam et al., 2014), CdSe quantum dot (Sun et al., 2008), CdTe nanorod (Li et al., 2010) have been already reported, but there is no study available for thiamine detection using C-dots as a probe. As C-dots are cheap, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly compared to the previously reported inorganic nanomaterials (Moghaddam et al., 2014; Li et al., 2010; Rao and Paria, 2014; Sun et al., 2008) it would be useful if C-dots have similar ability.

Herein, to the best of our knowledge, first time we are reporting the method for synthesis of highly fluorescent C-dots from the tender coconut water through microwave assisted hydrothermal method without addition of any other chemicals. The proposed route has many advantages over the reported studies via green routes (Table S1, )Supplementary material such as ease of synthesis, inexpensive precursor for carbon source, single step process, very fast, cost effective, hydrophilic in nature, monodisperse, highly stable without functionalization. The effects of temperature, as well as microwave irradiation time, were also studied to get different sized (<6nm) C-dots of tunable photoluminescent property. Furthermore, we are also proposing a new kind of “turn on” fluorescent sensor for thiamine detection using the as-synthesized C-dots. This analysis method is simple and cheap compared to the conventional chromatographic methods (GC or HPLC). Finally, these luminescent C-dots have also been used as a fluorescence probe for imaging of fungal cells to probe the applicability in bioimaging.

The hydrothermal synthesis was performed using a microwave (MW) reactor (StartSynth, Milestone) operated at the 800-W power supply. Each reaction was carried out in a sealed 25ml glass vessel. The actual temperature inside the vessel was maintained using an ATC Fiber optics sensor. A 10ml solution of coconut water and ethanol (1:1v/v) was used in the microwave reaction vessel for the synthesis of C-dots. Then the vessel was placed in the microwave and irradiated microwave at different temperatures for one min, the vessel was then cooled to room temperature and resulting the solution was centrifuged at 10,000RPM (revolutions per minute) for 10min to discard the large molecules.

Fluorescence measurement was carried out using a Hitachi spectrofluorometer (F-7000) with a slit width of 5nm for the excitation and emission. UV–vis spectroscopic measurements were recorded using a UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu-3600). FT-IR spectra were carried out in ATR mode (Thermo Fisher, Nicolet, iS-10). The size and shape of as prepared C-dots were observed under an HR-TEM (JEOL, JEM 2100F). X-Ray diffraction analysis was carried out using a Multipurpose X-Ray Diffraction System (Rigaku Japan/Ultima-IV) using Cu Kα radiation. The contact angle (θ) measurements were done on a glass plate by a video based contact angle meter, Dataphysics (OCA-20). Bright field and PL imaging were carried out using Leica (DM 2500M) fluorescence microscope. The Raman spectroscopy was carried out using a Triple Raman spectrometer (Horiba, T64000) equipped with 1800grooves/mm gratings, and Ar+ ion using 514.5nm exciting laser.

The quantum yields (QYs) of C-dots was investigated by comparing the integrated intensities (excited at 350nm) and the absorbance value of C-dots at 350nm with a reference. Quinine sulfate in 0.01M H2SO4 (QY=54%) was used as a standard sample; then the solution was diluted to five different concentrations in ultrapure water for the absorbance measurements. To minimize the reabsorption effects, absorption values of individual solutions were maintained below 0.1 at the excitation wavelength. The calculation of quantum yield was achieved according to the following equation:
(1)
QY
S
=
QY
R
(
Grad S
Grad R
)
×
(
η S
η R
)
2
where, QYS and QYR are the QYs of the sample and reference (Quinine Sulfate) respectively. GradS and GradR are the slopes of the integrate intensity vs. absorbance line for sample and reference. η S and η R are the refractive indexes of the sample and reference.

A 2ml of C-dots suspension was diluted in 98ml ultrapure water and incubated for 15min at room temperature. Then, CuCl2 solutions were added as a source of Cu2+ ions (concentration range 10–50µM) to the previously prepared C-dots suspension (denoted as C-dots-Cu2+). Next, an aqueous solution of thiamine was added to the suspension, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded after excitation at 367nm wavelength.

To test the selectivity towards other biologically relevant species present in human blood serum such as glucose and different ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Cl−,
SO 4
2 -
, and
NO 3 -
), the analysis was done in the presence of 100µM concentration of each of these species (4 times than thiamine concentration). The selectivity test was also done in the presence of artificial urine, which was prepared by following the reported composition (Brooks and Keevil, 1997). A mixture of 2.5mM CaCl2, 90mM NaCl, 2.0mM MgSO4, 10mM Na2SO4, 7.0mM K2HPO4, 1.1mM lactic acid, 2.0mM citric acid, 25mM NaHCO3, 170mM urea, and 25mM NH4Cl was made in ultrapure water. The final pH of the solution was adjusted to 6.0 by the addition of 1.0M hydrochloric acid. Finally, the analysis was also done in the presence of a mixture of all these compounds. To test the thiamine concentration in vitamin capsule (Becosules, Pfizer, multi-vitamin containing thiamine, vitamin B2 (riboflavine), vitamin B6 (pyridoxine HCI), vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin), niacinamide, calcium pantothenate, biotin, folic acid, and vitamin C), a capsule was dissolved in ultrapure water. The mixture was sonicated for 10min in a bath for fine dispersion, and then the fluorometric analysis was done as mentioned before.

The fungus (Aspergillus niger) was grown on the culture media. For bioimaging, grown fungal mass was simply kept in C-dots suspension at room temperature for one day. The cells were washed with ultrapure water to remove excess C-dots followed by centrifugation at 5000RPM for 10min. Finally, the fungus was placed on a glass slide with a cover slip and observed under fluorescence microscope. Similarly, the fluorescence imaging of C-dots, C-dots+Cu2+, and C-dots+Cu2++thiamine treated fungus cells were also done.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical characterization of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The physical appearances of the C-dots suspensions prepared at different temperatures were yellowish, brown, and dark brown color depending on the reaction temperature as shown in Figs. S1–4, Supplementary material. The tender coconut water is composed of sugars (sucrose, glucose, and fructose), vitamins, amino acids, phytohormones, and minerals (Yong et al., 2009). During the microwave irradiation, the carbonaceous natural compounds pass through the polymerization and carbonization processes sequentially and thereby fluorescent C-dots are formed. The mechanism of formation of photoluminescent C-dots involved in the microwave hydrothermal carbonization process is presented in Supplementary material (Scheme S1). The C-dots suspension was found to be remained stable for even 11 months without any settling or agglomeration of particles, as well as without change in PL intensity (Fig. S5, Supplementary material). To further optimize the PL performance of the C-dots, we have investigated the influence of reaction time and temperature. A constant microwave reaction time of 1min was maintained. Further, longer microwave irradiation (≥5min) and higher temperature (≥180°C) gives rise to the formation of agglomerate because of the high rate of carbonization and produced C-dots are non-fluorescent in nature.

The particle size and morphology of the C-dots were determined using an HR-TEM. The TEM images demonstrate that C-dots are mostly monodisperse and spherical in shape. The TEM images also show that a different distribution of C-dots at a different temperature. From the particle size distribution histogram based on TEM images shown in Fig. S6 Supplementary material, one can easily see that the average diameters are 2±0.5 (140°C), 4±1 (160°C), and 4±2nm (180°C). The HR-TEM images at 180°C show that the lattice parameters are 0.240 and 0.342nm, which correspond to the (100) and (002) facet of the graphitic carbon as shown in Figs. 1(d) and S7. However, the significant part of a particle is amorphous carbon structure, and a small portion is converted to the graphitic structure as a defect. This indicates the presence of structural defects in amorphous carbon or partially crystalline carbon.

The XRD analysis was made to get the information about the structure. The XRD pattern depicted in Fig. 2(a) shows a broad diffraction peak (2θ) at 23.79 (d 002=0.34nm), attribute to highly disordered carbon atoms, also there is a good agreement with the previously reported studies (Chowdhury et al., 2012; Jia et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2012). The XRD data supports that the as-synthesized particles are made of elemental carbon, as well as there is a good agreement with the result of HR-TEM lattice pattern. Noteworthy to mention that, when the precursor solution was heated for a longer time under the microwave (20min at 180°C), the formation of crystalline (graphitic) carbon was observed as shown in inset of Fig. 2(a).

The Raman spectroscopy was also done to support the hybridization state of the formed C-dots. The Raman spectra of C-dots at different temperatures (140, 160, and 180°C) clearly show two superimposed broad peaks of D-band (Sp3) and G-band (Sp2) at around 1400 and 1600cm−1, respectively, with fluorescence background (Fig. 2c–f). The presences of D and G bands in C-dots further support the Sp2 graphitic defect sites in Sp3 amorphous carbon. The Raman spectra of the sample heated for a longer time in the microwave (20min at 180°C) shows the higher degree of graphitization (Fig. 2f). These results clearly indicate that, with the increase in temperature or reaction time, gradually graphitization increases. Therefore, the Raman spectroscopic study confirms the carbonization of coconut water by microwave-hydrothermal process to the C-dots consisting of Sp3 carbons with Sp2 graphitic carbons as defects. These results are also consistent with the XRD and HR-TEM analysis.

Furthermore, the characteristic absorption band of surface functional groups of coconut water and C-dots were investigated by FT-IR analysis and shown in Fig. 2(b). It can be seen that the presence of –C–H (2940cm−1), –C=C (1406cm−1), –C–O (1068cm−1) bonds in coconut water. After carbonization, the strong and broad new peaks between 3200 and 3500cm−1 are because of the O–H (carboxylate and hydroxyl groups) or N–H vibrational stretching. In addition, sharp peaks at 1635 and ~1020cm−1 are because of the presence of –C=O and –C–O vibrational stretching shows the existence of a carboxylate and ether groups, respectively. The absorption peak at 2978cm−1 is because of –CH vibrational stretching of –CH3 and –CH2. While comparing all peaks before and after the carbonization, it has been found that there is an evolution of –OH, –C=O, and –NH groups on C-dots surface. The presence of various functional groups on the surface of C-dots can be used as a linker for specific binding of different biomolecules if those are used for biomedical applications.

To gain an idea about the development of the carbonization process, the UV–vis absorption spectra of C-dots synthesized at different temperatures (130–180°C) were measured. It can be seen that there is absorption maximum in UV region with a tail extending to the visible region. The absorption peak near 290nm, as shown in Fig. 3, is typically ascribed to the presence of aromatic π orbitals because of the formation of graphitic carbon structure (Hsu et al., 2012; Jia et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2012; Pan et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2012). The significant red shifting and increase in the intensity of the absorbance spectra was observed with the increase in size and carbonization with increasing temperature. To investigate further, the optical properties of as-synthesized C-dots at different temperatures, PL study was carried out at different excitation wavelengths. The PL study shows that the pure coconut water does not have any luminescence property, however, after microwave irradiation the C-dots show luminescence behavior as a function of reaction temperature and microwave exposure time, as shown in Fig. 4. The Fig. 4 depicts the emission spectra of as-synthesized C-dots at 130, 140, 150, 160, 170, and 180°C respectively; depending on synthesis temperature, they have a broad range of emission from UV (350nm) to visible (400–550nm) light. Despite the fact that, all samples exhibit excitation dependent emission spectra, but each sample has a most intense emission peak at a particular excitation. The C-dots synthesized 130–180°C (each 10°C interval) show their most intense emission peak at 346 (λ ex 290nm), 346 (λ ex 290nm), 426 (λ ex 350nm), 435 (λ ex 363nm), 460 (λ ex 380nm), and 519nm (λ ex 467nm), respectively. These results show PL properties of C-dots are highly dependent on their size and structure, which in turn can be tuned by changing the reaction temperature under the microwave. The size dependent PL is mainly because of quantum confinement; smaller the size leads to the larger band gap. The C-dots of 1–2nm size, synthesized between 130 and 140°C temperature ranges shows emissions in UV range. Whereas, little larger size particles (3–6nm) synthesized between 150–170°C and 180°C show blue and green light emission, respectively. The fluorescence quantum yield of C-dots synthesized at 180°C was found to be 2.8% measured in reference to quinine sulfate (Fig. S9, Supplementary material), which is comparable with the wide ranges of values (0.008–6%) reported before (Hsu et al., 2012; Jia et al., 2012; Krysmann et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2007, 2012; Peng and Travas-Sejdic, 2009; Wang et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2010a; Zhu et al., 2012). Based on our observation the emission properties of C-dots are originated because of two major reasons. Firstly, the generation of the band gap in C-dots is because of the presence of heterogeneous structure of Sp2 and Sp3 carbon, which in turn leads to discrete quantized energy levels (Cao et al., 2013; Krysmann et al., 2012; Loh et al., 2010). As a result, the optical transition of Sp2 localized electron–hole pair may initiate an emission in C-dots (Scheme S2, Supplementary material). Secondly, because of self-passivation of the particle surface by hydroxyl and carboxylic groups, which also lead to emissive surface traps and radiative recombination of excitons (Krysmann et al., 2012; Loh et al., 2010; Mitra et al., 2012; Sun et al., 2006). Thus, the key features of this synthesis method are tuning the carbonization temperature to regulate the C-dots particle size 1–6nm with tunable PL, because of the generation of more defect sites and carbonization.

The PL properties of C-dots prepared by simple hydrothermal method (in a furnace, in the absence of MW), and from the mature coconut water under MW are shown in Figs. S10 and S11 of Supplementary material for the comparision. It has been found that these two as-synthesized particles also have PL property, but the PL intensity is much lower, compared to that of MW synthesized C-dots from tender coconut. As microwave synthesized C-dots are more stable in aqueous media for a long time, and at the same time some previous reports mentioned that the hydrophobic nature of the C-dots (Mitra et al., 2012). To get an idea about the hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity of the C-dots, we measured the contact angle of water on the glass surface after coating with C-dots by sessile drop technique as shown in Fig. S12, Supplementary material. The contact angle of the water droplet increases 45–54° indicates the particles are slight hydrophilic in nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-dots based fluorometric detection of thiamine and selectivity test
ce:section.ce:para: To find the detection ability of thiamine, first, the PL spectra of C-dots under different conditions was investigated as shown in Fig. 5(a). It can be seen that the as-synthesized C-dots suspension exhibits a strong peak at 430nm wavelength, however, the peak intensity quenches in the presence of Cu2+ ions. When thiamine was added to the above C-dots-Cu2+ mixture, the fluorescence intensity enhances close to its original position. Then, to see the sensitivity of the method, different concentrations of thiamine in the range of 10–50μM were investigated. The PL spectra of C-dots-Cu2+ dispersion in the presence of different thiamine concentrations shows, that the PL intensity of the mixture increases with the increase of thiamine concentration as shown in Fig. 5(b). From the PL intensities, a linear standard calibration curve was obtained between ΔF(F−F 0) and concentration of thiamine as shown in Fig. 5(c) with correlation coefficient of >0.99. Where F 0 and F are PL intensities of C-dots-Cu2+ and C-dots-Cu2+-thiamine respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated from 3σ/m, where 3 is used for signal to noise ratio, σ is the standard deviation of blank measurement and m is the slope of the calibration curve. The calculated limit of detection is found to be 280nM, which is significantly low. In spite of the fact that, there are some NPs-based reported studies of lower LOD values (CdSe: 207nM (Sun et al., 2008), Y2O3:Eu:144nM (Moghaddam et al., 2014), and CdTe nanorod: 30nM (Li et al., 2010)) than this study, but our method is very simple, particles are nontoxic, synthesis step is very simple and fast, precursor is inexpensive. Since the thiamine concentration present in serum is in the range of 563-957nM (Rucker et al., 2001), so our LOD can be easily acceptable to determine thiamine concentration in blood sample. Therefore, the proposed sensing method is suitable for thiamine analysis in real samples. In addition, this method also provides an advantage in the pharmaceutical industry for detection of thiamine level. This method also exhibits low LOD value compare to the conventional chromatographic and voltammetric methods (Table S2, Supplementary material). On the other hand, C-dots based detection provids advantage over inorganic based material detection because of its simple, rapid, label-free, green, and cheap fluorescent sensing method without any modifications compared to the previously reported methods. Unlike the metal and semiconductor quantum dots, the synthesis of fluorescent C-dots is quite simple, facile, fast and economical, for trace analysis. Moreover, many metal and semiconductor quantum dots required complicated and time-consuming procedures, difficult to get small and mono-disperse sizes, expensive chemical precursor for the synthesis. Finally, there is no reported study so far for thiamine sensing through C-dots, which is the novelty of this study from the scientific view point.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed detection method of thiamine, the fluorescence response was investigated in the presence of artificial urine, glucose, and different inorganic ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Cl-,
SO 4
2 -
, and
NO 3 -
), which usually exist in blood and urine. As shown in Fig. S14(a) of Supplementary material, the restoration efficiency of thiamine is significantly higher than other molecules. The above results clearly demonstrated that this sensing method is highly selective toward the thiamine detection and does not suffer from interference by other molecules and ions present in urine and blood. To validate the applicability of thiamine biosensor for real samples, we tried to detect the thiamine concentration in a commercial vitamin capsule as shown in Fig. S14(b) of Supplementary material. As per the mentioned concentration on the capsule, 10 and 50μM concentrations were made for the analysis. From the fluorometric analysis we got the values of 0.503 and 1.149 and those form the linear calibration plot 0.518 and 1.116 for 10 and 50μM thiamine, respectively. The error in the analysis was found to be 2.89% and 2.87% for those two concentrations. This study indicates the proposed detection method can be applicable for real samples also.

The synthesized C-dots are used in this study to develop a novel “turn on” type fluorescent sensor for the thiamine analyte. The Cu2+ ions used in this study act as fluorescence indicator, which interact with C-dots (form a charge transfer complex) and quenches the emission intensity because of the non-radiative electron transfer from the excited states of C-dots to the d orbital of Cu2+ according to Eqs. (2)–(4) (Chan et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2012; Sha et al., 2013; Stanisavljevic et al., 2015; Yu et al., 2015; Zong et al., 2011). The as-synthesized C-dots contain –OH, –C=O, and –NH groups on the surface, as a result, the N and O containing surface groups of C-dots help complex formation with Cu2+ ions. Further, after the addition of thiamine as analyte, the Cu2+ ions displace from the C-dots surface because of formation of soluble Cu–thiamine complex in solution. In the previous studies it has been supported that the soluble Cu–thiamine complex formation occurs because of covalent bonding between Cu2+ ions and N (1′) of the pyrimidine ring (thiazole) of the thiamine molecule (Cramer et al., 1984; Fazakerley and Russell, 1975; Caira et al., 1974; Marzotto et al., 1970). Finally, when the Cu2+ ions displace out from the C-dots surface the PL intensity restore again as shown in Fig. 6(a).

To confirm the attachment and detachment of Cu2+, we investigated the zeta (ζ) potential measurements of the C-dots at different conditions. As shown in Fig. 5(d), the ζ-potential of C-dots shifted −10.7 to −2.64mV in the presence of Cu2+ ions. However, the ζ-potential value again increases to −8.06mV upon addition of thiamine, which indirectly supports the detachment of Cu2+ ions from the C-dots surface.

)
*

The main objective of this study is application of C-dots for thiamine detection, however, herein we present few preliminary results to show the applicability of this material for bioimaging. The fluorescence imaging ability of as-synthesized C-dots was evaluated on Fungus (A. niger) cells, eventually, it can also be used for any other cells for real applications. Fig. S15(a) and (b) shows there is no auto-fluorescence behavior of the fungal cells. However, when excited at 390nm wavelength, prominent blue emission was observed as shown in Fig. S15(c), while excited at 490nm wavelength, green emission was observed as shown in Fig. S15(d). These results clearly show that as synthesized PL C-dots are highly suitable for tunable bio-imaging. Further to demonstrate the fluorescent on–off process, the fluorescence imaging of C-dots, C-dots+Cu2+ and C-dots+Cu2++thiamine treated fungus cell are done and shown in Fig. 6(b–d). The images clearly show that in the presence of Cu2+ ions fluorescence emission is off and after addition of thiamine again it is on.

We report a facile, one pot microwave-hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent C-dots from tender coconut water within a minute. The particle size of the C-dots varies between 1 and 6nm depending on the microwave heating time and temperature. The synthesized C-dots exhibit blue and green light emission after excitation at 390 and 450nm wavelengths respectively. An optimum operating temperature shows small size, narrow size distribution, and better PL property of the C-dots. The PL property of the C-dots was utilized for the easy fluorometric detection of thiamine in aqueous solution, and it has been found that they are successfully able to detect thiamine to a minimum level of 280nM. The same procedure was used to detect thiamine concentration in a vitamin capsule as a real sample. The selectivity test shows that several inorganic and organic molecules expected to be present in urine and blood serum do not interfere on the analysis. This bio-molecular sensor is achieved based on the fact that Cu2+ ions quench fluorescence intensity of the C-dots and it recovers again after the addition of thiamine because of formation of soluble Cu–thiamine complex. The C-dots were also used for the bioimaging of fungal cells and it has been found the blue and green color fluorescence images are possible to capture while excited at 390 and 490nm wavelength lights respectively. So, the C-dots can be useful for the bioimaging application of other animal cells also.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, Kolkata, India for giving the opportunity to access their HR-TEM and Raman spectroscopy facility.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.12.087.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary material Supporting information that describes the formation of C-Dot, Quantum Yield, PL graphs, TEM images and Fungi cell bioimaging.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Marzotto, A., Nicolini, M., Signor, A., Galzigna, L., 1970. Physico-chemical studies on the structures of the thiamine–Cu (II) complex. Scientific repository. Messanae Universitas Studiorum, pp. 79–84.
